items.title: Governing AI agents in enterprise software engineering
items.description: Treat an autonomous coding agent as a privileged contributor, not a tool: scope its permissions, capture an auditable record of every change it proposes, and require a defined approval gate before its output reaches a protected branch or production. Bound the agent's blast radius (read-only by default, write only within a sandbox), make its reasoning and diffs reviewable, and keep a human accountable for the merge decision. The governance goal is reproducibility and traceability — any change the agent makes must be explainable and revertible.
items.applicability: Enterprise estates adopting AI-assisted or autonomous development where regulated change-control and audit trails are mandatory, including banking middleware.
items.example: An agent's fix-loop runs in an isolated workspace, emits a diff plus rationale, and a named reviewer approves before the change is applied — mirroring LocalAIFactory's guarded Workspaces scaffold and approval lifecycle.
items.limitation: Governance does not validate correctness; an approved-but-wrong change still ships. Pair approval gates with tests and observability, and never let agent volume outpace human review capacity.

items.title: Platform engineering and the internal developer platform
items.description: Platform engineering builds a curated, self-service internal platform (golden paths, paved roads, reusable templates) so product teams ship without re-solving infrastructure, security, and deployment each time. Treat the platform as a product with its own roadmap, users (developers), and success metrics. The win is reduced cognitive load and consistent guardrails baked into defaults rather than enforced by review.
items.applicability: Organizations with multiple teams repeating the same build/deploy/secure work, where standardization reduces risk and onboarding time.
items.example: A standard deployment template that bundles the runtime install, health checks, and config conventions so a new service inherits hardened defaults instead of bespoke scripts.
items.limitation: A platform imposed without developer buy-in becomes shelfware; measure adoption and developer experience, not just existence of the platform.

items.title: DORA four key metrics as a delivery health signal
items.description: Measure software delivery performance with four balanced metrics: deployment frequency and lead time for changes (throughput), plus change-failure rate and time-to-restore service (stability). Throughput without stability is reckless; stability without throughput is stagnant — the pairing is the point. Use the metrics to find systemic bottlenecks and guide investment, not to rank individuals.
items.applicability: Any team or estate wanting an evidence-based, comparable signal of delivery capability across systems and over time.
items.example: Tracking lead time from commit to production reveals a manual signoff step as the dominant delay, directing improvement effort at the process rather than at coding speed.
items.limitation: Metrics are derived from general industry research and are context-dependent; they describe systems, not people, and can be gamed if tied to individual incentives.

items.title: SRE error budgets to balance reliability and change
items.description: An error budget is the allowable unreliability implied by an SLO (e.g. a 99.9% target permits ~0.1% unavailability). When the budget is healthy, teams ship freely; when it is exhausted, change slows and reliability work takes priority. This converts the reliability-versus-velocity argument into a shared, data-driven policy rather than a recurring negotiation.
items.applicability: Services with explicit availability or latency targets where leadership must arbitrate between feature delivery and stability.
items.example: A spike in change-failure rate burns the error budget, triggering an agreed freeze on non-critical changes until reliability is restored.
items.limitation: Error budgets require a meaningful SLO and trustworthy measurement; a budget set on a metric users do not care about gives false comfort.

items.title: SLO and SLI design that reflects user experience
items.description: Define service level indicators (SLIs) from the user's perspective — success rate, latency at a chosen percentile, freshness — then set service level objectives (SLOs) as realistic targets on those indicators. Good SLIs are measurable at the request boundary and correlate with actual user pain. Avoid vanity metrics (CPU, raw uptime) that can look green while users suffer.
items.applicability: Any service where reliability must be quantified to drive prioritization, on-call policy, and stakeholder communication.
items.example: An SLI of 'percentage of page loads completing under one second' aligns directly with LocalAIFactory's rule that core pages must return quickly, making the target observable.
items.limitation: Percentile choice and measurement window materially change the picture; document them. An SLO is a target, not a contractual guarantee unless explicitly stated as an SLA.

items.title: Secure-by-design and shift-left security
items.description: Build security into design and the earliest development stages rather than bolting it on before release. Shift-left means threat modeling at design time, secure defaults, dependency and secret scanning in the pipeline, and developer-owned remediation close to the point of change. Defects caught early are cheaper and less disruptive than those found in production or audit.
items.applicability: Regulated and high-assurance estates where security debt is expensive and late discovery threatens release schedules.
items.example: A pipeline that fails the build on a known-vulnerable dependency or a committed secret stops the issue before it reaches a protected branch.
items.limitation: Shift-left tooling produces false positives that erode trust if unmanaged; tune signal-to-noise and keep a path to risk-accept findings with sign-off.

items.title: Observability-first operations (logs, metrics, traces)
items.description: Design systems so their behavior is explainable from the outside using structured logs, metrics, and distributed traces. Observability-first means instrumentation is a build-time requirement, not an incident-time scramble: every request is traceable, key paths are timed, and anomalies surface before users report them. The aim is to ask new questions of a running system without shipping new code.
items.applicability: Distributed or long-running services where failures are emergent and root-cause analysis must be fast.
items.example: Request-timing middleware that logs a start and a completion line per request makes a hung endpoint visible as a 'started' with no matching 'completed', pinpointing a stall.
items.limitation: Telemetry has cost and cardinality limits; over-instrumentation can overwhelm storage and obscure signal. Sample deliberately and protect sensitive fields.

items.title: Human-in-the-loop AI governance
items.description: Keep a competent human accountable at decision points where AI output carries material risk. Human-in-the-loop means the model proposes and a person disposes for high-impact actions, with the human given enough context (confidence, evidence, alternatives) to decide well rather than rubber-stamp. Reserve full automation for low-risk, reversible, well-bounded actions.
items.applicability: Banking and other high-stakes domains where automated decisions must remain explainable, contestable, and accountable.
items.example: An AI-flagged document anomaly is routed to a reviewer with the model's confidence and the supporting evidence, separating detection from the human verification decision.
items.limitation: Token human oversight (approving faster than one can read) provides governance theater, not control. Match review depth to risk and measure override rates.

items.title: Safety practices for AI-assisted development
items.description: AI-generated code and suggestions are drafts to be verified, not authoritative output. Safety practices: run the same build, test, and security gates on AI contributions as on human ones; never let an assistant introduce unreviewed dependencies or secrets; constrain context so the model cannot leak sensitive data; and keep the developer responsible for understanding what they merge. Trust the process, not the generation.
items.applicability: Teams using code assistants or autonomous agents inside a controlled SDLC, especially with proprietary or regulated codebases.
items.example: An assistant proposes a query rewrite; it is accepted only after the existing runtime checks confirm the page still returns quickly and no large text column is materialized.
items.limitation: Plausible-looking AI code can hide subtle defects and licensing issues; review for correctness and provenance, not just compilation.

items.title: Knowledge management as a curated, approved asset
items.description: Organizational knowledge gains value when it is curated and approved rather than merely accumulated. Capture decisions, rules, and patterns with provenance and a review state, retire what is stale, and inject approved knowledge first into work contexts so teams build on trusted ground. The discipline is lifecycle management: capture, review, approve, version, and expire.
items.applicability: Long-lived estates and platforms that must retain institutional knowledge across staff turnover and accumulate it over time.
items.example: An approval lifecycle that promotes a rule from draft to approved and injects approved knowledge into every prompt context — the core pattern behind LocalAIFactory's project memory.
items.limitation: Unreviewed knowledge bases decay into noise; without an owner and an expiry policy, captured 'knowledge' misleads as often as it helps.

items.title: Engineering leadership: outcomes over output
items.description: Effective engineering leadership steers toward business and user outcomes rather than raw activity. Set clear intent and constraints, push decisions to the people closest to the work, remove blockers, and protect focus. Measure whether the work changed reality (a metric moved, a risk retired) rather than whether tickets closed. Leadership creates the conditions for good engineering, not the code itself.
items.applicability: Teams and estates where leaders must allocate scarce capacity and align technical work with stakeholder value.
items.example: Prioritizing a stabilization fix that keeps core pages loading over a new feature, because reliable rendering is the outcome stakeholders actually depend on.
items.limitation: Outcome focus can neglect necessary invisible work (maintenance, security) that has no immediate visible result; budget for it explicitly.

items.title: Innovation delivery: discovery before scale
items.description: Innovation delivery separates cheap, fast discovery (validating that an idea is desirable, viable, and feasible) from expensive scaling (hardening and rolling out the validated idea). Run small, time-boxed experiments with clear kill criteria; invest in scale only after evidence. This limits the cost of being wrong while keeping a path open for being right.
items.applicability: Organizations introducing new capabilities (such as local AI assistance) where uncertainty is high and over-investment in unproven ideas is a real risk.
items.example: Piloting an AI-assisted fix-loop on a contained workspace with explicit success criteria before committing to estate-wide rollout.
items.limitation: Endless experimentation without a decision gate becomes avoidance; set criteria to either scale, pivot, or stop.

items.title: Change management for technical rollouts
items.description: Technical change succeeds or fails on the human and process change around it. Manage change by communicating the why, sequencing rollout in reversible steps, training affected operators, and providing a rollback and support path. Record approvals and a clear go/no-go decision. The goal is that a change lands predictably and can be undone without drama.
items.applicability: Production rollouts in controlled estates where unplanned change causes incidents and audit findings.
items.example: A deployment drill that rehearses install, validation, and rollback before the real change window, so the team executes a known sequence rather than improvising.
items.limitation: Heavy change process can throttle delivery; right-size ceremony to risk, and reserve full rigor for high-impact or irreversible changes.

items.title: Stakeholder signoff and decision records
items.description: Material decisions need an explicit, attributable signoff that records who approved what, on what evidence, and with what conditions. Signoff converts implicit assumptions into an accountable agreement and provides the audit trail regulators and operators rely on. Capture the decision, the alternatives considered, and any accepted risk at the moment of approval, not retrospectively.
items.applicability: Regulated delivery where ownership of risk and the basis for a go-live must be demonstrable after the fact.
items.example: A handover package that captures a named approver's signoff on a release candidate alongside the validation evidence that supported it.
items.limitation: A signature does not transfer competence; ensure the approver actually has the context and authority, or the record is hollow.

items.title: Pilot-to-production transition discipline
items.description: Moving from pilot to production is a deliberate transition, not a quiet ramp-up. Define exit criteria for the pilot (evidence of value, acceptable failure modes, operability), then harden the pilot's shortcuts: replace manual steps with automation, add monitoring and alerting, document runbooks, and confirm support ownership. A pilot proves desirability; production demands operability.
items.applicability: New capabilities graduating from a contained trial to estate-wide, supported use.
items.example: An AI-assist pilot is promoted only after its workspace guardrails, observability, and operator runbook meet defined production-readiness criteria.
items.limitation: Pilots accumulate hidden manual scaffolding; failing to inventory and replace it leads to fragile production systems that 'worked in the pilot'.

items.title: Incident and postmortem culture (blameless)
items.description: A blameless postmortem culture treats incidents as system signals, not personal failures, so people share what really happened. After a significant incident, reconstruct the timeline, identify contributing causes (usually several), and produce tracked, owned actions that reduce recurrence. The output is organizational learning and a measurably more resilient system, not a scapegoat.
items.applicability: Any operations organization that wants reliability to improve over time rather than repeat the same failures.
items.example: A page-hang incident yields a recorded root cause (a group-by-constant aggregate) and a prevention rule banning the pattern, turning one outage into a durable guardrail.
items.limitation: Blameless does not mean accountability-free; actions must still be owned and completed, or the culture decays into ritual write-ups with no follow-through.

items.title: Engineering for high-volume scaling
items.description: Scaling for high volume is about removing the dominant bottleneck, not adding capacity everywhere. Profile under realistic load, find the constrained resource (a lock, a slow query, an external call on the request path), and address it before scaling out. Prefer asynchronous and batched work for spiky load, cache stable reads, and keep slow dependencies off the synchronous path. Measure, then scale.
items.applicability: Systems facing growth in users, data, or request rate where naive scaling masks rather than fixes contention.
items.example: Reading service health from a cached snapshot instead of probing external services per request keeps page latency bounded as traffic grows.
items.limitation: Premature scaling adds complexity and cost; scale in response to measured demand, and re-profile after each change because the bottleneck moves.

items.title: Architecture decision records (ADRs)
items.description: An ADR captures a significant architectural decision: the context, the options considered, the choice made, and its consequences. ADRs are short, immutable once accepted, and superseded rather than edited. They give future contributors the reasoning behind the system so they can change it knowingly instead of reverse-engineering intent. The record is the asset; the decision becomes traceable history.
items.applicability: Any system expected to outlive its original authors, where the rationale for key choices must survive staff turnover.
items.example: Recording the decision to keep MSSQL as the primary store with Qdrant and Ollama optional, including the consequence that no page may depend on an external service to render.
items.limitation: ADRs add value only if written at decision time and kept discoverable; retrofitted or buried ADRs are rarely consulted.

items.title: Risk acceptance and explicit risk ownership
items.description: Not every risk can or should be eliminated; some are knowingly accepted. Risk acceptance makes that choice explicit: document the risk, its likelihood and impact, the compensating controls, the accepting owner, and a review date. This prevents silent risk accumulation and gives the organization a register it can revisit as conditions change. Accepting a risk is a decision, so record it like one.
items.applicability: Regulated estates where residual risk must be owned, justified, and periodically re-evaluated rather than ignored.
items.example: Accepting that a security scanner finding is a false positive, recorded with a named owner and a review date, so the acceptance is auditable and time-bounded.
items.limitation: Acceptance without a review date becomes permanent blindness; risks change, so every accepted risk needs an expiry and re-assessment trigger.

items.title: Operator handover and runbooks
items.description: A clean handover transfers not just software but the ability to operate it. Provide runbooks for routine and failure scenarios, the validation commands that prove health, the known failure modes and their fixes, and clear escalation paths. The test of a handover is whether a competent operator who did not build the system can run, diagnose, and recover it from the documentation alone.
items.applicability: Releases transferred from a build team to an operations team or customer, common at go-live and customer handover.
items.example: A handover walkthrough that lists the per-page health checks an operator runs to confirm every core page returns quickly after deployment.
items.limitation: Runbooks rot as systems change; tie runbook review to release cadence or they describe a system that no longer exists.

items.title: Change-approval gates and protected branches
items.description: A change-approval gate is the control point where a proposed change must pass defined checks (review, tests, security, approval) before it can affect a protected branch or environment. Gates make 'who can change what, under what conditions' explicit and enforceable. Keep gates automated where possible so they are consistent, and reserve human approval for judgment that machines cannot make.
items.applicability: Estates requiring controlled, auditable change to production-bearing branches and environments, including AI-agent contributions.
items.example: An autonomous agent's change is blocked at a protected branch until a named reviewer approves the diff and automated checks pass.
items.limitation: Gates add latency; too many or redundant gates slow delivery and tempt bypasses. Design the minimum set that actually reduces risk.

items.title: Reproducible builds and supply-chain integrity
items.description: A trustworthy release is reproducible and its provenance is verifiable. Pin dependency versions, build from a clean known source, generate a software bill of materials (SBOM), and record checksums so a deployed artifact can be tied back to its inputs. Supply-chain integrity means you can answer 'what is in this build and where did it come from' — essential when a dependency is later found vulnerable.
items.applicability: Regulated and security-sensitive deployments where artifact provenance and dependency transparency are required.
items.example: Publishing checksums alongside a release package so an operator can verify the artifact they deploy matches the one that was approved.
items.limitation: An SBOM lists components but does not by itself assess their risk; pair it with vulnerability monitoring and an update plan.

items.title: Cloud-native and CNCF operating patterns (applied judiciously)
items.description: Cloud-native patterns — declarative configuration, immutable infrastructure, automation, and loosely coupled services — improve repeatability and recovery even when not running on a public cloud. Adopt the principles (everything reproducible from source, infrastructure as code, automated rollout and rollback) selectively; a local-first estate benefits from declarative, scripted, idempotent operations without needing a full orchestration platform.
items.applicability: Teams wanting reproducibility and automation benefits while running on constrained, on-premises, or air-gapped infrastructure.
items.example: Scripting deployment and rollback idempotently so a local IIS host can be brought to a known state without manual steps, applying cloud-native discipline without cloud dependence.
items.limitation: Importing heavyweight cloud-native tooling into a small local estate adds operational burden without payoff; take the principles, not necessarily the platforms.

items.title: Continuous delivery with trunk-based flow and small batches
items.description: Delivering in small, frequent, independently shippable batches reduces risk and shortens lead time. Short-lived branches integrated often (trunk-based flow), feature flags to decouple deploy from release, and a fast automated pipeline mean each change is small enough to reason about and revert. Large, infrequent releases concentrate risk and make failures hard to isolate.
items.applicability: Teams aiming to improve DORA throughput and stability simultaneously by limiting work-in-progress and integration distance.
items.example: Shipping a stabilization fix as its own small change that can be validated against the core-page health checks and rolled back independently if needed.
items.limitation: Small batches require automated tests and quick gates to be safe; without them, frequent change merely increases the rate of unverified failures.

items.title: Technical debt management as a deliberate ledger
items.description: Technical debt is a tool when taken knowingly and a liability when accrued silently. Manage it as a ledger: record deliberate shortcuts, their cost of carry (slower change, higher defect risk), and a plan or trigger for repayment. Reserve recurring capacity for paydown so debt does not compound into a system nobody can safely change. The decision to incur debt should be as explicit as the decision to repay it.
items.applicability: Long-lived systems and legacy modernization where unmanaged debt eventually halts safe delivery.
items.example: Marking a guarded-off scaffold as deliberate, time-bounded debt with a documented activation plan rather than leaving its status ambiguous.
items.limitation: Not all messy code is 'debt' worth tracking; over-cataloguing trivia wastes effort. Track debt that measurably slows change or raises risk.
